speaker: interviewer
text: One, two, three, one. It's February the 20th, 1996. The survivor is Sam Mathis. Carol Stulberg, interviewer. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English and in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg, S-T-U-L-B-E-R-G. I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Sam Mathis in Los Angeles, California, on the 20th of February, 1996. Good morning, Mr. Mathis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sam Mathis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Szulim ?] [? Moshe ?] Kashemacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the name on your birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get that name? Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: When you're born, they give you the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Mucemacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mucemacher. M-U-C-E-M-A-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that you have a different name than your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother divorced my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was five years old, maybe. And then she didn't like keeping-- keeping his name. [INAUDIBLE], I don't know. Then she [INAUDIBLE] her name, Kashemacher. And everybody know in the city my name is [? Szulim ?] Kashemacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scheindel Kashemacher

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born August the 8th, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city, what Country

speaker: interviewee
text: In city, Radzyn Podlaski, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today? Today, I'm 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, where was Radzyn Podlaski near? What big city was it near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]. [? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] from the other side, and [INAUDIBLE] on the other side-- [? four ?] sides. Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one sister. Dvora Riwka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dvora-- Dvora Riwka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she older or younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three-- she was three years younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your father did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to make fur coats. Oh, very, very pretty ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as you said, when you were about five years old, your parents divorced.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you and your sister live with just your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work to support you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother had to work, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind work. All kind. Is not like here in United States. They can't-- they don't give you any benefit. You have to work hard. And she work hard. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? I tell-- my mother's brother, he left-- he left-- he left to Johannesburg. His name was [? Johan ?] [INAUDIBLE]. He was a good tailor in the city. He left to Johannesburg. Then he was in Johannesburg, in Africa. He was a tailor there. Then if you don't like there, you come back. And then he said to my mother, I used to go to school before. I used to go to Hebrew school. And she pay for this. Not free. Until later on, after I finish the school, Hebrew school, [? she went to ?] the public school. Polish public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he come back. And my father-- my-- my uncle comes back from Africa. He said to my mother, why you have to learn-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: He broke my heart. Let him go. I'm going to teach him about tailor. Then I started to go to for a tailor. And all the time, they used to ask me, the boys, my friend, why you come back to school? When you come back this? I said, tomorrow, tomorrow. That's happening. I never got back. How old were you when you started to learn how to be a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you started to learn to be a tailor at the age of 13, what do you remember about your childhood and school? Where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember-- not good-- not very good. What is there to remember? I-- I never want to remember. I don't like to remember any more. I suffer a lot. [CRYING] See, that is all I think, [INAUDIBLE] before, cause I got nothing [INAUDIBLE] it. No. No. That's happened. And I get, you know, older, and started to work and learn a trade, and I started to make money. Then my-- then I said to my mother and it broke her heart. And my sister started to work. She used to make bras-- how you call it-- for-- for a woman. Very good. And she started to work, too. [CRYING] And then, that is happen the trouble. And comes the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we start talking about the war, let me take you back just a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what kind of a religious background you had at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother used to-- religious-- we-- we are Jews and religious, very religious. No. Yeah, she never said [INAUDIBLE] Saturdays, you cannot do it Fridays, Saturdays, not in Europe, difference. She-- first of all, they never had electric over there. How you call it? Gas-- night-- different lamps, you know, you have to in, not gasoline. How you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Kerosene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kerosene. That's-- that's always life in Poland. Later on, when I started to work, it was different, all different. Changed then. Not for a long time. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not for long, because after that, we were not far from the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you remember about celebrating Jewish holidays as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. You have to go some-- Yom Kippur. Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah. Saturdays, my mother, even she left [? nothing ?] [INAUDIBLE]. She [INAUDIBLE] every Friday. [CRYING] They come. Men-- used to go, in Poland, used to go [INAUDIBLE]. Poor men used to go for-- go around to every house, to give them a penny like this. She go home, and go back, and take some of their own for eat. Friday night. Even she works hard. She was-- she was very good. [CRYING] That I can't ever forget it. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's life. While you were in school, did you have a lot of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. No, not Polish, most Jewish, all Jewish. Even the public school, only Jews. No. Poland was not so good. Polish were anti-Semite all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Poland, terrible. I used to belong to [NON-ENGLISH]. We used to go [INAUDIBLE], all the group. I used to a stick in my hand, put it here, because I'm afraid of the Polish. It started before [? the war started, ?] the Nazis. And the youth in Poland, we used to call them [NON-ENGLISH]. And the Nazis, they call. Oh, boy. One time I used to go to work to my uncle, wasn't in-- in lunchtime, after we come home, going back, they run after me, with the Gestapo-- the Polish [NON-ENGLISH] with knives in hand. And I ran so fast. I ran-- if I don't run, they will kill me. Poland's not good. The worst-- worst country for. I never want to go back after the war because of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities did you like to do as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a young boy. I used to belong to a sport club. Make sport, and then we make-- I don't know what they call it-- [INAUDIBLE] used to make-- not as a young man, not heavy, skinny. I used to go on the top for somebody else in the-- in the-- in a pyramid. Then I belonged-- here called soccer. Here, they call it soccer. In Europe, they called it football, different. I do a lot of things. I used to make, like, make pictures, too, like I do now. I used to make sometime, used to belong-- used to think, I can write good. I used to print-- how you call  it, [NON-ENGLISH], how you call it in English-- on the streets, to make it, used to [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know how to call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Signs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Signs. Yeah, used to do this. Oh, yes. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you turned 13 years old, you went to work. You started to learn how to work. Did you actually have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about starting to work? What kind of work did you do, and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did with my uncle. I started-- [INAUDIBLE] started to tailor. You have to start-- [? I cannot ?] explain it to you. To start being a tailor is not so easy. People think here, tailors are easy. I have had tailor shop. This not-- here, even Los Angeles, I'm not a tailor like this. I'm a custom tailor. [? I help-- ?] movie stars [INAUDIBLE]. I show you my shop here. I've got a little-- you see [? a ?] movies [INAUDIBLE]. These come here in the house. I quit to come to do them. Plus, after the war. You [INAUDIBLE] I'm talking about the war. Yeah, I always forget. My uncle was the best tailor in the city. Everybody know this. And he said to me, he never-- [CRYING] Oh, I cannot talk [INAUDIBLE]. And goes back and back, then I see [INAUDIBLE]. It's not too good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you started working, you helped your mother with money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things get a little better for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You know the picture I show before, the people that are in Israel, my mother used to go work for them, too. She used to come, said, your mother will leave now-- [CRYING] She was a [INAUDIBLE]. She started to cry, too. She said to me, if your mother would leave now, [NON-ENGLISH]. They love her. She was-- she was never-- [CRYING] oh, let me talk something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us-- tell us about the Jewish community in the city where you lived. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not like here. [INAUDIBLE]. No. Those Jews were [INAUDIBLE]. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a large community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I can show you. The said with 4,000 people there, more than just Jewish. That was a small town [INAUDIBLE]. I got [INAUDIBLE]. Was after the war, the other [INAUDIBLE]-- how you call, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's over 4,000 gone and dead in concentration camp. Lot of them-- lot of them-- mostly Jewish. Most [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in your city altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never think to pay attention. I didn't pay attention. [INAUDIBLE] I don't care. Here, you care how many. In Europe, you don't care how many people. You got your friend, you got a [NON-ENGLISH]. Who care how many people? Mostly-- mostly Gentiles used to live out-- out of the city. Most. Oh, I don't care how many. I never pay attention to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned before that you were a member of Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you used to do in that organization? What kind of activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they used to-- used to send it out of [NON-ENGLISH], they call the camps the summertime, together. Used to go [NON-ENGLISH]. Used to go all the time in meetings together, and talk about Israel, talk everythings. They-- [INAUDIBLE] Israel. Oh, yeah. Every time. Going dance, all the time. Every-- every-- after work, right after work, we used to go to it. They called it, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what to call it in English. I don't care because there's no organization [? here ?] too much. It's not like Europe. If you want to see people, you have to go to the temple. Or you got today, the [INAUDIBLE] club, the Survivor Club, that's it. That's the only things you can go. Otherwise, you have to sit in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was an opportunity for you to meet with other young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. All of them. You see how many people show in the picture. There was richer, and the poor men. They like me to go. I was a poor man. [CRYING] I was poor and honest. Yeah. That's [? happen. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something. In about 1933, when Hitler came to power, did you hear anything about it in your city in Poland? Did you know anything that was going on in Germany at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We read papers. I used to read the [NON-ENGLISH]. I used to go to work. Used to work for other people, too, not just from my uncle. Later on, used to go to other-- other tailors. In the morning, I used to-- before I go to work, I used to read in paper, [? foreign ?] [? ones. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was it written in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. Yiddish. All Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all kind thing-- other things in and other wars that started before. And then I-- one time I left the [INAUDIBLE], because I cannot work no more in the-- in home. I left [INAUDIBLE] to work. I was in [INAUDIBLE] about few months. My mother had a brother in [INAUDIBLE], too. Then they started the war Poland starting the war-- Germany against-- I was over there. I forgot the name of it. [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, hold on. Czechoslovakia. [NON-ENGLISH]. Before the war. I was in [INAUDIBLE], working over there. Then my uncle, other uncle, said to me, go home. My mother will-- then I left back home. And that's it. They started wars with Poland, others. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old were you when you first heard about the trouble in Germany and Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans [INAUDIBLE] 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember as being the first signs of trouble in your city? How did things change? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. When Hitler come. Oh, boy. Then it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Just before Hitler. Just before Hitler. Do you remember anything that changed before the Germans actually invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism. It was bad. Oh, there-- I saw-- a lot of Polish used to stand by the Jewish stores and never intending to buy something against the Jews. Oh, that's [? terrible. ?] [INAUDIBLE] don't go to the Jews, don't go to the Jews, don't go to the Jews. It's just terrible. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, in 1939, when the Germans came into Poland, what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember? Is how right away, they run to every house and look for boys, for men, for men, for men-- go to work. Take me to work. All the time, right away. [INAUDIBLE]. You never can sleep any more. I was afraid. After-- after-- a while, [INAUDIBLE], they started curfew time. Then it was worse then. You cannot go out. After 6:00, you have to stay in the house. That was worst thing for them to catch us. You cannot go out. It's just trouble. [INAUDIBLE] [? think. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to work in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning, yes, [? off that bat. ?] Oh, then they started. The [INAUDIBLE] the Polish [INAUDIBLE] do the thing. Then they started to put on a band with a-- with a Jewish star. And I used to go-- I used to take the shirts and cover this. Then the Polish said, he's a juden. He's a juden. He's Jewish, Jew. That was terrible. Terrible. Nothing to hide, no place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the conditions change for you in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worse. For everybody. Not just for me, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: What way? What they-- then the Germans try to make a-- call up all the Jews, the richer Jews, and they make them to be the leaders. If they need something, they have to-- they have to get it from the others. Oh, then it's trouble. Then it started trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in other words, what you're saying is that the Germans appointed Jewish leaders to lead the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a name for this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: All those names-- I-- I remember the names. Because mostly they take the richer people. I remember some names. [INAUDIBLE], other names, oh, [INAUDIBLE]. Yes. Oh, some people was good, and some was bad. Even they was richer, some was good and some was bad. They take advantage of the-- of the poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: What way? Did they need some to work? They take the poor people. I remember, some time ago, the poor children [INAUDIBLE]. They called it the Judenrat. In Jewish, they call the Judenrat. Here, they call it the-- I don't know how they call it. They used to go over there and-- and cry, why you send us for work? [INAUDIBLE] others, you think the others are gonna stay alive? They used to hit them. Oh boy. This was not no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any experience with the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, experience, because they want to take me to work all the time. And I used to work with my uncle. My mother said, no, you not going to work. He's my husband. [CRYING] They sent my-- my sister-- my sister to work. She said-- because if she goes, [INAUDIBLE]. They're going to hit me. My sister always used to call for me. She's not-- [CRYING] [INAUDIBLE]. I never forget it. [CRYING] My sister. [CRYING] She [INAUDIBLE]. If I ever go, sometimes take away, you never come back. And even though I said, no, I want for him to go, she said no, he's not going. You should fight. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] See, that's why I [? never ?] liked to act, said come like this still. I got too much trouble. [CRYING] I don't know. Then another thing with the Judenrat-- how call-- how you call the unit-- the [? committee, ?] [? committee ?] [INAUDIBLE]. They do something else, or they started to [INAUDIBLE] trouble. Then I work later on with my uncle. [INAUDIBLE] never [? can sometimes ?] [? get on ?] not good. Then I went to other place work. [INAUDIBLE] what used to belong to the community-- leaders from the-- from the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Judenrat. And I asked him, why you take [INAUDIBLE]? [INAUDIBLE] the paper from the German Gestapo, and then after take me to work. No, you don't do it. He did it for his friends and family. Not the family. He's [INAUDIBLE] other state, other city. Then I said, what to do? I said. I don't know. I got no other choice. I quit. Because I don't like, like this. I went back to my uncle. My uncle is the best tailor. He used to work for the Gestapo. Every things. The best suits. And they said-- used to send it back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I work for him long time. [INAUDIBLE] all the time to make a transport out. [? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] ?] Over there was the main-- main-- the main stop to send [INAUDIBLE] Auschwitz, Treblinka, other-- other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said, long time I [INAUDIBLE] on the list. There were [INAUDIBLE]. Let me go back. It used to catch [INAUDIBLE] to work. Long time. I know they knocking the door, in the night, [INAUDIBLE]. I went to my cousin. Because I told you, lived in our city-- in our place, [INAUDIBLE]. Went the middle-- in going-- [INAUDIBLE]. In the morning, on the hill-- on the [INAUDIBLE] cover. And I said, you sit up. She sit up [INAUDIBLE] covered. [INAUDIBLE] family. That's how I hide myself like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there actually a ghetto formed in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not-- not-- was not close. [INAUDIBLE]. Jews was together, one place. They don't go out because [INAUDIBLE] close. They took away all the big-- big houses, the newer houses, for the army. Just there were side streets just for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see after there, they-- there are other times, they do-- they used to get people to work. Not to work. [INAUDIBLE] we never come back. A long time after, I see the [INAUDIBLE]. I said to Mother, what can I do now? Then I said to Mother, [INAUDIBLE] the floor or [INAUDIBLE] on the floor. She covered it all-- took off in the bed, [INAUDIBLE] the floor, to show the people, the Germans, there was other Germans already messed us up. And I was underneath, [? in the barn. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when they went out, I said to Mother, come on. I dress in for a woman. My sister [INAUDIBLE]. My mother's-- and I walk out. Another place, I work the assembly line. I work-- I work the assembly place. Around December, [INAUDIBLE] a friend. I learned to use-- on-- on-- on a roof. A roof attic like this, is in over there two days, two days. In a heat like this, it's summertime. Oh, boy. After a while, they said, they already-- they already left. You come down. I had too much trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you in this attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, he's-- he's there. [? Zelig-- ?] his name was [? Zelig ?] [INAUDIBLE]. His sister lives in Israel. Yeah. She lives there. Course, I ask him in the Russian-- the Russian was not far from our city. I went to him south. I said, [? Zelig, ?] maybe you go with me to Russian? He said, [INAUDIBLE]. Go on. Go on. Go away. Don't go. He not going. And what is happened? Course he used to work for the Germans, too, [? and so on. ?] [INAUDIBLE] nothing, [INAUDIBLE] nothing. He worked. He worked if they needed him [INAUDIBLE]. OK. No good. Yeah, that's nothing. Later on, long time, I got [INAUDIBLE] on the girls. A lot of things happened. Sitting-- that was a Saturday-- Saturday or Sunday, Sitting in our home, and talking, talking. Then I see, come Sunday, The [INAUDIBLE], the Gestapo, they get on the-- there [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH], they call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: A skull.

speaker: interviewee
text: To catch the Jews. Oh, boy. Then I run off my attic, where I used to-- run, you know, outside was a ladder, and up and down. And he saw me going down. He said, come [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm gonna kill you. I got no choice. I go down. Then he hold a rifle, like this, run, go. Then I go. He said, [INAUDIBLE]. [? He laugh ?] at me. They were coming on our street. I remember the city was [INAUDIBLE]. There my-- my friend from [INAUDIBLE], they started talking about the girls. They said to me, [? Szulim, ?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I hid it. They shot after me. They cannot catch me, and I run out. you were running and this-- Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Gestapo was shooting at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I run with [? Zelig. ?] Then I hide, because I know the city. I was living there. And I hide. And my mother ran after me. And she-- she was so close to him. [INAUDIBLE]. And I hide in a-- in a shack. He cannot find me. Then after a while, my mother said to me, she won't-- she [? won't ?] talk to me. [INAUDIBLE] different name. And I know what she said. Then I run out, go over-- over the roof to another place. And go to my girlfriend, I used to go with her. And then her brother-in-law's in our basement around him. And after all, they run out in our city, this-- [? they said, ?] oh, he's still alive. I was alive. My mother doesn't believe it yet, but there's not [INAUDIBLE]. Then one night, everything else happened in not in same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then that little door it open, open, never opens. They come in. They go to my sister. She was laying in bed with my mother, taking off the shirts. They take me, they [INAUDIBLE]. How you call it? The police [NON-ENGLISH]-- cane. With a club.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the club. They knock my sister. They knock my mother. And I was in other room. [INAUDIBLE]. I [INAUDIBLE]. I run out, and they run after me. And I run of-- of the fields. Across a gardens there, and run over there, to my-- to my cousin, different city, used to live on a gentile street. I went there. And I said, [INAUDIBLE]-- this is a cousin, [INAUDIBLE]. She-- I said, my mother been dead. [INAUDIBLE]. And then he covers it. We cover [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE]. My mother comes in the morning, and looks, and see they are still alive. And she cries. And I cry. [INAUDIBLE] to me. And then start over [? close, ?] and [? close. ?] And we come-- and I see my mother. In the night, they knocked in the door to get everybody out. She said, well, no, [INAUDIBLE]. Maybe yes. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] yes. [DRINKS] I'm all get dry when I'm excited too much what's happened. Going back so many years. Long time happen was less than [INAUDIBLE]. Then I make-- I make in a house another [INAUDIBLE], poor house. Was a suitcases, everything's to [INAUDIBLE] floor. Was a bed. I, with my cousin, my other cousin, dig a hole every night. We make this-- here-- here was a table. Here was a bed. And you opened all under-- under the table. Then ran under the bed. Then make hole [INAUDIBLE]. Catch the Jew, the boys. They ran around to the barn. Cover this. And cover this, nobody will notice. After all this, come to learn, too, they said they have to make a [INAUDIBLE]. Long time, and they knock the door, and took us all away. Oh, yeah, there's not the girls. They did-- the Judenrat, [? you see, ?] the-- the-- the community. Because they have to get the Germans, how many people for a transport. [INAUDIBLE] Gestapo, to Treblinka and other places. They pick people that they like. You know, they cannot afford to pay me. They figured they got more money, maybe they can buy you off, not to go. They put my mother on the list. I look my mother on the list. I said to my partner, to my uncle, I cannot see my [INAUDIBLE] sister.
